The second experiment aims to assess the predictive performance of RePHINE in combination with GNNs on popular benchmarks for graph classification.
All methods were implemented in PyTorch, and our code is available at https://github.com/Aalto-QuML/RePHINE.
We consider three datasets of cubic graphs (or 3-regular graphs): cub08, cub10, and cub12.
These graphs cannot be distinguished by 1-WL and color-based PH as all vertices share the same color. Thus, we modify the datasets by changing the colors of 1, 2, or 3 vertices in each graph sample.
Also, we randomly partition each dataset and create a balanced binary classification task.
We compare standard 0-dim persistence diagrams from vertex-color filtrations to 0-dim RePHINE (i.e., no missing holes).
Both approaches are processed using DeepSets with exactly the same structure and optimization procedure.
Also, they operate on the original colors, not on GNN embeddings.
For completeness, we report results for a 2-layer graph convolutional network (GCN) followed by an MLP.
We are interested in assessing if the persistence modules can overfit the observed graphs.
Graphs can be overfitted when the observed data is fitted too closely, potentially leading to poor generalization.

The expressivity of methods is evaluated based on the proportion of unique graph embeddings over training.

Our results suggest that RePHINE should be the default choice for persistence descriptors on graphs as GIN-based approaches achieve slightly better results.
Accuracy results on graph classification show that RePHINE performs better than PersLay

RePHINE+Linear method is compared against PersLay method in terms of leveraging GNNs. Their initial designs are adapted for fair comparison by computing RePHINE diagrams with learned filtration functions and applying a linear classifier to provide class predictions. Additionally, vectorial representations of the RePHINE diagrams are concatenated with the graph-level features obtained using PersLay.

RePHINE+Linear method is a variant that incorporates RePHINE diagrams with learned filtration functions. It achieves higher accuracy compared to other methods based on the results obtained from 5 runs on 4 datasets.

RePHINE introduces a novel topological descriptor based on persistent homology methods for graph representation learning. It is computationally efficient and capable of distinguishing attributed graphs with general node- and edge-color filtrations.
Persistent homology methods are used to establish a complete characterization of attributed graphs distinguishable with general node- and edge-color filtrations in graph representation learning.
Similar to how WL test has advanced GNNs, the framework of color-separating sets introduced in the context enables the design of more powerful topological descriptors like RePHINE.
RePHINE can be readily integrated into GNNs, yielding empirical gains on several real benchmarks.
We have not analyzed here other types of filtrations, e.g., those based on the spectral decomposition of graph Laplacians.
